concerns.text: Assumption about inference framing states: 'all findings... will be framed as associational, not causal'. However, the project goal is to 'distinguish between two competing physical theories' (Photoevaporation vs. Core-Powered Mass Loss) which are causal mechanisms. The proposed methodology (observational regression of gap slope) cannot causally distinguish these mechanisms without controlling for confounding variables (e.g., stellar age, metallicity, multiplicity) that drive both the gap and the period distribution. A correlational design is insufficient to validate the causal claims of the theories.

FR-007 mandates a Monte Carlo simulation comparing measured slope to theoretical distributions. However, the spec fails to account for the confounding effect of detection completeness. The 'Assumption about predictor collinearity' mentions applying a completeness correction as a covariate, but the regression model (FR-006) is defined only as 'gap radius vs log(period)'. Without explicitly modeling the completeness map as a predictor or weighting the regression by the inverse of the detection probability, the measured slope will be biased by Malmquist bias, invalidating the comparison to theoretical slopes.

The plan proposes merging bins with <30 planets to meet a power threshold. This introduces a selection bias where the final regression only includes bins that happen to have high planet counts (likely due to higher detection efficiency or specific stellar populations). This 'survivorship bias' in binning invalidates the assumption that the resulting slope represents the true underlying physical relationship across the full 5-100 day range.

FR-004 requires a minimum peak separation of ≥ 0.1 R_earth to confirm bimodality. This arbitrary threshold lacks statistical justification relative to the measurement uncertainties of the Kepler data. If the true gap is narrower than 0.1 R_earth but statistically significant given the data precision, this rule will force the model to treat a bimodal distribution as unimodal, leading to Type II errors in gap detection and biasing the slope calculation.
